title: The Staging Area
intro: A developer just finished building a login feature but also has some unrelated debug files lying around. They want to commit ONLY the login files, but their command stages everything. Find the bug.

teach.title: The Staging Area = Your Shopping Cart
teach.body: Imagine you're at an online store. You browse around, adding items to your cart. But you don't buy everything in the store -- you only check out with the items you actually want. Git's **staging area** works the same way. You pick exactly which changes go into your next commit.

teach.title: Why Not Just Commit Everything?
teach.body: Sometimes you've changed 5 files, but only 3 of them are related to the feature you're working on. The other 2 are debug code, notes, or experiments. The staging area lets you **commit only what makes sense together** and leave the rest for later.

teach.title: Different Ways to Stage Files
teach.code:
git add .                  # Stage ALL changed files
git add style.css          # Stage one specific file
git add src/login/*        # Stage all files in a folder
git add *.js               # Stage all JavaScript files
teach.annotation: `git add .` is convenient but dangerous when you have files you don't want to commit. Be specific when it matters.

teach.body: The staging area lets you choose exactly which changes go into each commit -- use specific paths with `git add` instead of blindly adding everything.

code.text: # Stage only the login feature files

exercise.prompt: The developer only wants to commit login files, not the debug files. Tap the line that causes the problem.
exercise.explanation: The command 'git add .' stages ALL changed files in the current directory and below -- including those debug files. When you only want specific files, use a path like 'git add src/login/*' to stage just the files you need. Think of it like putting only certain items in your shopping cart instead of dumping the whole shelf in.
